title.en: Taking the unglamorous piece because the team needed it
title.es: Tomar la parte poco glamorosa porque el equipo la necesitaba

question.en: Describe a time you took on boring, unglamorous, or career-limiting work because the team needed it. How did you feel about it in the moment, and what actually happened?
question.es: Describe una ocasión en la que asumiste trabajo aburrido, poco glamoroso o que limitaba tu carrera porque el equipo lo necesitaba. ¿Cómo te sentiste en el momento, y qué pasó en realidad?

must_include: the specific work the candidate took on — named concretely: legacy data pipeline cleanup, test coverage for an inherited codebase, documentation that nobody wanted to write, migration glue code, on-call rotation no one was willing to own

why it was boring or career-limiting — the candidate should be able to name what made it unglamorous: no architectural novelty, not visible to leadership, would not appear on a resume, was someone else's mess
who benefited from the candidate taking it — the named teammate or team function that was unblocked or enabled by the candidate doing this work

an honest account of how the candidate actually felt — not a polished retrospective ('I knew it was the right call') but what the in-the-moment experience was; flat affect ('it was fine') is more credible than performed nobility
the actual outcome — whether anyone noticed, whether it mattered, whether the team shipped because of it; including the possibility that it went largely unnoticed is acceptable and adds credibility
must_avoid: martyrdom or sacrifice framing — 'I gave up my own growth to help the team' signals resentment and frames the contribution as moral performance rather than team-oriented action
retroactive nobility — 'I always believe in doing the unglamorous work' as a general principle rather than a specific instance with a real cost and a real beneficiary
minimizing the unpleasantness ('it wasn't that bad, really') — if the work wasn't actually unglamorous, this is not the story; the whole point is acknowledging the real cost
framing where the candidate negotiated a trade ('I took the boring work in exchange for getting Track A next sprint') — conditional contributions are not ST; they are deals
